draft.introduction: Euripides’s Bacchae (405 BCE) dramatizes the arrival of Dionysus at Thebes and King Pentheus’s fatal resistance. The play is the most important literary source for understanding Dionysiac religion and the dangerous power of divine possession.

translation.text: O blessed is he who, being fortunate and knowing the rites of the gods, makes his life holy and joins his soul to the sacred band, dancing in the mountains with holy purifications, celebrating the mysteries of great mother Cybele.
translation.original_text: ὦ μάκαρ, ὅστις εὐδαίμων τελετὰς θεῶν εἰδὼς βιοτὰν ἁγιστεύει καὶ θιασεύεται ψυχάν

translator_notes.note: The choral ode’s macarism (“blessed is he”) parallels the Eleusinian formula. Euripides presents Dionysiac worship as simultaneously beautiful and terrifying—the same god who brings ecstatic joy tears Pentheus apart. This ambivalence toward divine possession runs through all Greek mystery religion.
